text: I'd like to make a system which turns any webpage or file into an accurate markdown version of itself

That sounds like a powerful utility for standardizing messy web content. I've assumed this is intended for internal staff to clean up documentation or research—does that sound right, or are you aiming for a public-facing tool?
I want it to be accessible to anyone in the APS but not just in my org

Opening this up to the entire APS is a great way to scale the impact, but it introduces significant security and authentication requirements. How do you plan to handle access control and ensure that sensitive documents uploaded by one agency aren't accessible to users from another?
I don't know? I don't think i'd need to retain any of the information. It's just a converter

If the system is strictly ephemeral—processing the file and immediately purging it from memory—that significantly lowers your security risk profile. If you're not storing data, how will you handle the inevitable 'oops' moments, like a user needing to re-download a file they just converted or reporting a failed conversion?
I think that's a risk for the user to own not me. As far as the failed conversion goes, I'd expect it to be robust enough to not fail

Relying on user-side persistence is a clean way to keep your architecture stateless, but even the most robust LLM will occasionally hallucinate or fail on complex layouts. If you aren't providing a history or retry mechanism, how will you handle user feedback when the AI produces a 'broken' or inaccurate Markdown file?
Who said anything about an LLM?

Fair point—I jumped to an LLM-based solution, but there are plenty of deterministic ways to handle document conversion. If you're avoiding AI, are you looking at a rules-based parser or a library-driven approach like Pandoc, and how does that shift your expectations for handling complex or non-standard layouts?
